critic_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A regional grocery chain trained a gradient-boosted demand forecasting model for weekly unit sales across 600 store-SKU pairs. The model uses three years of history and predicts one week ahead. Inputs include lagged sales, holiday indicators, local weather, planned promotions, and whether an item is temporarily displayed on an endcap. The team evaluates on the most recent 20 weeks and reports weighted absolute percentage error of 11.8%, compared with 18.9% for the baseline. The baseline is described as the retailer's current seasonal heuristic: forecast next week's sales using sales from the same week last year. The paper emphasizes that the new model is particularly strong around holidays and promotion periods, where the error gap widens further. Buried in the methods section, the authors state that the heuristic baseline does not ingest the promotion calendar or endcap schedule even though those plans are available before forecast time and are part of the model inputs. They also mention that the retailer's current heuristic was designed for categories with relatively stable pricing and was never re-parameterized after the chain expanded from 18 to 34 stores, increasing promotion frequency. The authors argue that this is the operational baseline and therefore the relevant benchmark. They additionally report that all model features are known before the week begins and that prediction latency is negligible because forecasts are computed overnight. The operations team wants to use the reported results to justify replacing the chain's replenishment planning logic before the next holiday season.

CRITIQUE:

This evaluation has three significant methodological problems that undermine the reported performance gap.

1. BASELINE MISSING KNOWN EXOGENOUS INPUTS: The baseline heuristic deliberately excludes the promotion calendar and endcap schedule, even though these are fully known before forecast time. The same information is provided to the GB model. This is not an apples-to-apples comparison — the reported 7.1 pp WAPE gap is at least partly an input-information gap, not a model-quality gap. A fair comparison would require either augmenting the baseline with the same promotional inputs or using a simple promotion-adjusted seasonal naive (e.g., multiply seasonal average by a promotion lift factor derived from historical data). The emphasis on holiday and promotion periods as the main win zone makes this especially suspicious: that is precisely where the information advantage matters most.

2. BASELINE NOT RE-ESTIMATED AFTER REGIME CHANGE: The chain expanded from 18 to 34 stores, increasing promotion frequency substantially. The heuristic was never re-parameterized after this expansion. This means the baseline is not just simpler — it is actively miscalibrated for the current regime. Comparing a tuned modern model against a stale, miscalibrated heuristic inflates the apparent advantage. A properly maintained seasonal benchmark fit on the available three-year window would provide a more honest reference.

3. NO STRONGER STATISTICAL BASELINE: Neither a promotion-aware seasonal naive nor any statistical time-series model (e.g., SARIMA, exponential smoothing with exogenous inputs) is included as an intermediate baseline. The paper jumps directly from a degraded heuristic to a full gradient-boosted model. It is unknown whether a well-specified statistical model with promotional inputs would close most of the gap. This omission means the contribution of gradient boosting per se is unquantified.

Conclusion: The 37% relative WAPE improvement is substantially inflated by the information disadvantage imposed on the baseline and by the baseline's regime miscalibration. The model may still be genuinely superior, but the evidence as presented does not cleanly support the claimed magnitude of improvement.
defender_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. A regional grocery chain trained a gradient-boosted demand forecasting model for weekly unit sales across 600 store-SKU pairs. The model uses three years of history and predicts one week ahead. Inputs include lagged sales, holiday indicators, local weather, planned promotions, and whether an item is temporarily displayed on an endcap. The team evaluates on the most recent 20 weeks and reports weighted absolute percentage error of 11.8%, compared with 18.9% for the baseline. The baseline is described as the retailer's current seasonal heuristic: forecast next week's sales using sales from the same week last year. The paper emphasizes that the new model is particularly strong around holidays and promotion periods, where the error gap widens further. Buried in the methods section, the authors state that the heuristic baseline does not ingest the promotion calendar or endcap schedule even though those plans are available before forecast time and are part of the model inputs. They also mention that the retailer's current heuristic was designed for categories with relatives stable pricing and was never re-parameterized after the chain expanded from 18 to 34 stores, increasing promotion frequency. The authors argue that this is the operational baseline and therefore the relevant benchmark. They additionally report that all model features are known before the week begins and that prediction latency is negligible because forecasts are computed overnight. The operations team wants to use the reported results to justify replacing the chain's replenishment planning logic before the next holiday season.

DEFENSE:

This study is methodologically sound and the comparison against the operational baseline is appropriate and practically relevant.

1. OPERATIONAL BASELINE IS THE RIGHT BENCHMARK: The seasonal same-week-last-year heuristic is the system currently making replenishment decisions. Comparing against what is actually deployed — not an idealized version of it — is the correct approach for a replacement decision. The operations team needs to know whether deploying the new model improves on existing practice, not on a hypothetical augmented heuristic that doesn't exist. Using the live operational baseline is standard in applied forecasting work.

2. MODEL USES ONLY PRE-AVAILABLE FEATURES: The paper confirms all features are known before the forecast week begins and that batch latency is negligible. There is no leakage. The promotional inputs are legitimate because they represent planned calendars available in advance.

3. EVALUATION WINDOW IS REPRESENTATIVE: Twenty weeks of evaluation spanning multiple seasons and promotional cycles is a reasonable holdout for a weekly forecasting task with 600 store-SKU pairs, providing over 12,000 test observations. The holiday and promotion emphasis reflects where the model adds the most real-world value.

4. WAPE IS AN APPROPRIATE METRIC: Weighted APE handles scale differences across high- and low-volume SKUs correctly and is standard in retail forecasting.

The study provides sufficient evidence to support a controlled pilot deployment, even if future work should include an augmented baseline for completeness.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises three distinct issues: (1) the baseline lacks promotional/endcap inputs that were available before forecast time; (2) the baseline was never re-parameterized after a significant regime change (store expansion doubling promotion frequency); (3) no intermediate statistical baseline with exogenous inputs was tested. The defender argues the operational baseline is the right comparator for a replacement decision and that the model's features are all pre-available. The defender's point about operational relevance has merit for a go/no-go deployment decision, but it does not negate the logical problem: the claimed WAPE gap is partly attributable to information asymmetry between model and baseline, not purely to model quality. The paper explicitly uses the result to quantify the model's superiority, which requires an information-controlled comparison. The regime miscalibration point (store expansion, never re-parameterized) is uncontested by the defender. The absence of any promotional-aware statistical baseline means the degree to which gradient boosting itself contributes is unknown. These are genuine analytical flaws that inflate the reported performance differential.
